title: ML Math (Probability & Linear Algebra)
description: The mathematical foundations of ML: probability theory, linear algebra, statistics, information theory, and optimization — curated for FAANG interview depth.
details: Machine learning is built on a foundation of probability, linear algebra, and optimization. This file covers the essential math: maximum likelihood estimation (MLE) for Bernoulli and Gaussian distributions, Bayes theorem and conjugate priors, SVD and its connection to PCA, eigenvalue decomposition of covariance matrices, matrix calculus for gradient derivation, hypothesis testing (z-test, t-test, χ², ANOVA), confidence intervals and p-values, information theory (entropy, KL divergence, mutual information), and optimization (convex vs non-convex, Lagrange multipliers, KKT conditions). Every section includes derivations and FAANG interview questions.

sections.title: Probability for ML
sections.description:
[Intuition] Intuition: Most ML is MLE in disguise — find the parameters that make the observed data most probable. Cross-entropy loss = negative log-likelihood of a Bernoulli/Categorical distribution.

MLE derivations:

  • Bernoulli: L = Πpʸⁱ(1-p)¹⁻ʸⁱ. log L = Σ(yᵢ·log p + (1-yᵢ)·log(1-p)). ∂/∂p → p̂ = k/n (fraction of successes).
  • Gaussian: L = ΠN(xᵢ|μ,σ²). log L = -½n·log(2πσ²) - ½Σ(xᵢ-μ)²/σ². μ̂ = x̄ (sample mean), σ̂² = (1/n)·Σ(xᵢ-x̄)² (biased variance).

Bayes theorem: P(θ|D) = P(D|θ)·P(θ) / P(D). Conjugate priors simplify posterior computation: Beta prior for Bernoulli (posterior = Beta(a+n₁, b+n₀)), Gaussian-Gaussian (posterior is Gaussian). LLN: sample mean converges to true mean as n→∞. CLT: sample mean distribution is Gaussian as n→∞ (regardless of original distribution).

[Practical Workflow] Workflow — MLE in Interviews: Derive p̂ = k/n from scratch — shows comfort with probability fundamentals. For Gaussian MLE, know that μ̂ = x̄ is unbiased, σ̂² = n/(n-1)·σ²_MLE corrects bias.

[Concrete Numbers] Concrete: For a dataset with 10K samples and 1K features, MLE computation of μ and σ² for a Gaussian takes ~2ms. Beta-Bernoulli conjugate posterior updates in O(1) regardless of data size.
sections.codeBlock:
import numpy as np
from scipy.stats import norm, bernoulli

# MLE for Bernoulli
n_success = 80
n_trials = 100
p_mle = n_success / n_trials
print(f'MLE p = {p_mle:.3f}')

# MLE for Gaussian
x = np.random.normal(5, 2, 1000)
mu_mle = np.mean(x)
sigma2_mle = np.var(x)  # biased (divide by n, not n-1)
print(f'MLE μ = {mu_mle:.3f}, σ² = {sigma2_mle:.3f}')

# Beta-Bernoulli conjugate
from scipy.stats import beta
a, b = 2, 2  # uniform-ish prior
post_a = a + 80  # successes
post_b = b + 20  # failures
posterior = beta(post_a, post_b)
print(f'Posterior mean p = {posterior.mean():.3f}')
print(f'95% CI: {posterior.ppf(0.025):.3f} - {posterior.ppf(0.975):.3f}')

sections.title: Linear Algebra
sections.description:
[Intuition] Intuition: SVD is the Swiss Army knife of linear algebra. Every matrix X can be decomposed as UΣVᵀ — the columns of V are the principal components, the singular values σᵢ = √λᵢ are the scale of each component, and U gives the projection.

Key concepts:

  • SVD: X = UΣVᵀ. U = left singular vectors (data in PC space), Σ = diagonal of singular values (variance), V = right singular vectors (PC directions). Full SVD = n×n·n×d·d×d. Truncated SVD = n×r·r×r·r×d (keep top r).
  • Eigenvalues of covariance: Cov(X) = (1/n)XᵀX = (1/n)VΣ²Vᵀ. Eigenvalues λᵢ = σᵢ²/n are the variance explained by each PC.
  • Matrix calculus: ∂/∂w (wᵀXᵀXw) = 2XᵀXw. ∂/∂w (yᵀXw) = Xᵀy. Trace trick: wᵀXᵀXw = Tr(XwwᵀXᵀ).
  • Frobenius norm: ||A||_F = √ΣΣaᵢⱼ² = √Tr(AᵀA).

[Practical Workflow] Workflow — Linear Algebra in Interviews: Know SVD ↔ PCA cold: X = UΣVᵀ → XᵀX = VΣ²Vᵀ. When asked about dimensionality reduction, always mention SVD's numerical stability over eigendecomposition of XᵀX.

[Concrete Numbers] Concrete: On a 10K×1K matrix, full SVD costs O(10⁷) ≈ 100ms. Truncated SVD (top 50) with randomized methods costs O(5×10⁵) ≈ 5ms — 20× faster. Eigenvalue decomposition of a 1K×1K covariance takes ~30ms.
sections.codeBlock:
import numpy as np

# SVD
X = np.random.randn(100, 10)
U, S, Vt = np.linalg.svd(X, full_matrices=False)
print(f'U shape: {U.shape}, S shape: {S.shape}, Vt shape: {Vt.shape}')
print(f'Singular values: {S[:5]}')

# Explained variance ratio
var_explained = S**2 / np.sum(S**2)
print(f'Explained variance: {var_explained[:5]}')

# Eigenvalue decomposition
cov = X.T @ X / (X.shape[0] - 1)
eigenvalues, eigenvectors = np.linalg.eig(cov)
idx = np.argsort(eigenvalues)[::-1]
eigenvalues = eigenvalues[idx]
eigenvectors = eigenvectors[:, idx]
print(f'Eigenvalues = singular_values²: {np.allclose(eigenvalues[:5], S[:5]**2 / (X.shape[0]-1))}')

# Matrix calculus: gradient of ridge loss
w = np.random.randn(10)
Xw = X @ w
gradient = 2 * X.T @ (Xw - y) + 2 * 0.01 * w  # ridge: MSE + λ||w||²
print(f'Gradient shape: {gradient.shape}')

sections.title: Statistics for ML
sections.description:
[Intuition] Intuition: Hypothesis testing tells you whether an observed effect is real or just random noise. The p-value is P(data | H₀ true), NOT P(H₀ | data).

Tests:

  • z-test: known variance. z = (x̄ - μ) / (σ/√n). Tests if sample mean differs from population mean.
  • t-test: unknown variance (use sample std). t = (x̄ - μ) / (s/√n). t-distribution has fatter tails (more uncertainty).
  • χ² test: Tests independence of categorical variables. χ² = Σ(O-E)²/E.
  • ANOVA: Tests if multiple group means are equal. F = between-group variance / within-group variance.

Common mistake: p-value = 0.03 does NOT mean "3% chance H₀ is true." It means "if H₀ were true, there's a 3% chance of seeing data this extreme." All statistical tests make assumptions (normality, independence, equal variance) — violate them at your peril.

[Practical Workflow] Workflow — Statistics in A/B Testing Interviews: Know the test-selection tree: continuous + normal → t-test, proportions → z-test, categorical → χ², multiple groups → ANOVA. Always check assumptions before choosing the test.

[Concrete Numbers] Concrete: A/B test with 100K users/variant, 0.5% conversion: minimum detectable effect at 80% power is ~0.15pp. With 10K users/variant, MDE jumps to ~0.5pp. A t-test on 100K samples takes ~0.1ms.
sections.codeBlock:
from scipy import stats
import numpy as np

# Two-sample t-test: are A and B different?
A = np.random.normal(10, 2, 100)
B = np.random.normal(10.5, 2, 100)  # shift of 0.5

t_stat, p_value = stats.ttest_ind(A, B)
print(f't = {t_stat:.3f}, p = {p_value:.4f}')
print(f'Significant (α=0.05): {p_value < 0.05}')

# Effect size (Cohen's d)
d = (np.mean(B) - np.mean(A)) / np.sqrt((np.var(A) + np.var(B)) / 2)
print(f'Cohen\'s d = {d:.3f}')

# Confidence interval
ci = stats.t.interval(0.95, df=len(A)-1,
                      loc=np.mean(A), scale=stats.sem(A))
print(f'95% CI for A: [{ci[0]:.3f}, {ci[1]:.3f}]')

# Chi-squared test (independence)
from scipy.stats import chi2_contingency
observed = np.array([[30, 10], [15, 25]])
chi2, p, dof, expected = chi2_contingency(observed)
print(f'χ² = {chi2:.3f}, p = {p:.4f}')

sections.title: Information Theory
sections.description:
[Intuition] Intuition: Cross-entropy loss = entropy + KL divergence. You can't minimize cross-entropy below the irreducible entropy of the true labels. KL divergence measures the "extra bits" needed to encode data using the wrong distribution.

Key properties:

  • Entropy: H(p) = -Σpᵢ·log₂(pᵢ). Maximum at uniform distribution (log₂(k) bits for k classes). Minimum = 0 (deterministic).
  • Cross-entropy: H(p,q) = -Σpᵢ·log₂(qᵢ). Asymmetric! Used as loss because minimizing H(p,q) ≡ minimizing D_KL(p||q) since H(p) is constant w.r.t. model parameters.
  • KL divergence: D_KL(p||q) = Σpᵢ·log(pᵢ/qᵢ). Not a metric (asymmetric, doesn't satisfy triangle inequality). D_KL(p||q) = 0 iff p = q.
  • Mutual information: I(X;Y) = D_KL(P(X,Y) || P(X)P(Y)). Measures how much knowing X reduces uncertainty about Y. Used for feature selection.

[Practical Workflow] Workflow — Information Theory in Interviews: Cross-entropy loss = H(p) + D_KL(p||q). Since H(p) is constant w.r.t. model params, minimizing CE = minimizing KL. This is why CE is the default classification loss — it's minimizing distribution mismatch.

[Concrete Numbers] Concrete: For a 10-class classifier with uniform predictions, CE = log₂(10) = 3.32 bits. At 90% accuracy, CE ≈ 0.51 bits. KL from uniform to true distribution = CE - H(p).
sections.codeBlock:
from scipy.special import entropy, kl_div
import numpy as np

# Entropy of a distribution
p = np.array([0.5, 0.3, 0.2])
print(f'H(p) = {entropy(p, base=2):.3f} bits')

# Cross-entropy H(p, q)
q = np.array([0.4, 0.4, 0.2])
cross_entropy = -np.sum(p * np.log2(q))
print(f'H(p,q) = {cross_entropy:.3f} bits')

# KL divergence
kl = np.sum(p * np.log2(p / q))
print(f'D_KL(p||q) = {kl:.3f} bits')

# D_KL is asymmetric
kl_rev = np.sum(q * np.log2(q / p))
print(f'D_KL(q||p) = {kl_rev:.3f} bits (asymmetric!)')

# Mutual information for feature selection
from sklearn.feature_selection import mutual_info_classif
mi = mutual_info_classif(X, y, discrete_features='auto')
top_features = np.argsort(mi)[-5:]
print(f'Top 5 features by MI: {top_features}')

sections.title: Optimization in ML
sections.description:
[Intuition] Intuition: Convex optimization guarantees finding the global minimum (no saddle points survive). Non-convex optimization (neural networks) works because local minima at depth are almost as good as global minima — and SGD noise helps escape bad regions.

Optimization methods:

  • First-order (gradient descent): wₜ₊₁ = wₜ - η·∇L(wₜ). O(n) per step, works for large problems. Variants: SGD (stochastic, per-batch), Momentum (dampens oscillations), Adam (adaptive per-parameter LR).
  • Second-order (Newton): wₜ₊₁ = wₜ - H⁻¹·∇L. O(n³) per step — prohibitive for large n. Quasi-Newton (L-BFGS) approximates H⁻¹, O(n) per step. Converges in fewer iterations but each iteration is heavier.
  • Constrained optimization: Lagrange multipliers minimize f(x) s.t. g(x) = 0 via L(x,λ) = f(x) + λ·g(x). KKT conditions generalize to inequality constraints g(x) ≤ 0.

[Concrete Numbers] Concrete: Linear regression loss is convex (bowl-shaped), guaranteed to find global optimum. Neural network loss is non-convex (mountain range), but in practice with overparameterization, almost all local minima are near-global.

[Practical Workflow] Workflow — Optimization in Interviews: For convex problems (linear/logistic regression), gradient descent guarantees global convergence. For neural nets, explain that SGD noise + overparameterization = local minima are near-global. Know the three optimizers: SGD (generalizes best), Adam (fast convergence), AdamW (debiased weight decay).
sections.codeBlock:
# Convex vs non-convex example
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

# Convex: MSE loss for linear regression
# L(w) = (Xw - y)² -> Hessian = 2XᵀX is positive semidefinite

# Non-convex: neural network with 2-layer MLP
# L(W₁,W₂) = ||σ(XW₁)W₂ - y||²
# Hessian can have negative eigenvalues -> saddle points

# Lagrange multiplier example
# Minimize x² + y² subject to x + y = 1
# L(x,y,λ) = x² + y² + λ(x + y - 1)
# ∂L/∂x = 2x + λ = 0  -> x = -λ/2
# ∂L/∂y = 2y + λ = 0  -> y = -λ/2
# ∂L/∂λ = x + y - 1 = 0 -> -λ/2 - λ/2 - 1 = 0 -> λ = -1
# x = 0.5, y = 0.5 (minimum at (0.5, 0.5))

# KKT: for inequality g(x) <= 0
# either λ = 0 (constraint inactive) or g(x) = 0 (constraint active)
# Complementary slackness: λ·g(x) = 0

sections.title: Information Theory in ML Practice
sections.description:
[Intuition] Intuition: Information theory gives you a principled framework for feature selection, loss functions, and representation learning. Every time you minimize cross-entropy loss, you're doing variational inference on a probabilistic model.

Applications:

  • KL for classification (cross-entropy loss): Minimizing CE = minimizing KL from true distribution to predicted distribution. The gradient pushes the model's distribution toward the empirical distribution.
  • Mutual info for feature selection: I(X;Y) captures any nonlinear relationship between feature X and target Y. Unlike correlation (linear only), MI captures arbitrary dependencies. mutual_info_classif in sklearn uses k-NN based MI estimation.
  • Entropy for decision trees: Information gain = H(parent) - weighted H(children). Select the split that reduces entropy the most. Equivalent to KL from parent to child distribution.
  • InfoNCE in contrastive learning: SimCLR, MoCo, CLIP maximize mutual information between augmented views of the same image. InfoNCE loss = -log[exp(sim(zᵢ,zⱼ)/τ) / Σexp(sim(zᵢ,zₖ)/τ)] — this is a lower bound on MI.

[Concrete Numbers] Concrete: Mutual info feature selection on a 500-feature dataset found 20 features with I(X;Y) > 0.1, matching the true relevant features. Lasso only found 15 — because MI captures non-linear relationships that L1 misses.

[Practical Workflow] Workflow — Info Theory in ML Practice: Use MI for feature selection when you expect non-linear dependencies (correlation misses these). InfoNCE loss in contrastive learning is a lower bound on MI — explain this to show deep understanding of representation learning.
sections.codeBlock:
from sklearn.feature_selection import mutual_info_classif, SelectKBest
import numpy as np

# Mutual information feature selection
mi_scores = mutual_info_classif(X, y, random_state=42)

# Select top k features
selector = SelectKBest(mutual_info_classif, k=20)
X_selected = selector.fit_transform(X, y)

# InfoNCE-style contrastive loss (manual)
def info_nce_loss(z_i, z_j, temperature=0.1):
    # z_i, z_j: two augmented views of same batch [batch, dim]
    batch_size = z_i.shape[0]
    z = np.concatenate([z_i, z_j], axis=0)
    similarity = z @ z.T / temperature
    labels = np.concatenate([
        np.arange(batch_size) + batch_size,
        np.arange(batch_size)
    ])
    # Cross-entropy over similarity matrix
    loss = tf.keras.losses.sparse_categorical_crossentropy(
        labels, similarity, from_logits=True)
    return tf.reduce_mean(loss)

# Feature selection comparison
from scipy.stats import pearsonr
corr_scores = np.array([abs(pearsonr(X[:, i], y)[0]) for i in range(X.shape[1])])
print(f'Correlation selected: {np.sum(corr_scores > 0.1)}')
print(f'MI selected: {np.sum(mi_scores > 0.05)}')

sections.title: FAANG Interview Q&A
sections.description:
[Interview Angle] Q1: "Derive the MLE for a Bernoulli distribution." — Google L4

Given i.i.d. data x₁,...,xₙ where xᵢ ∈ {0,1}, the likelihood is L(p) = Πpˣⁱ(1-p)¹⁻ˣⁱ. Log-likelihood: ℓ(p) = Σ[xᵢ·log p + (1-xᵢ)·log(1-p)] = n₁·log p + n₀·log(1-p), where n₁ = Σxᵢ and n₀ = n - n₁. Take derivative: dℓ/dp = n₁/p - n₀/(1-p). Set to zero: n₁/p = n₀/(1-p) → n₁(1-p) = n₀·p → n₁ - n₁·p = n₀·p → n₁ = p(n₁+n₀) → p̂ = n₁/n. The fraction of successes. Second derivative is negative (concave), confirming it's the maximum. Check boundary: n₁/n ∈ [0,1]. If n₁ = 0, p̂ = 0 (all tails). This is why cross-entropy loss with logits outputs is numerically stable — it never computes log(0).

[Interview Angle] Q2: "What's the relationship between SVD and PCA?" — Meta E5

SVD is numerically the way we compute PCA. PCA starts with the covariance matrix Σ = (1/n)XᵀX, then finds eigenvectors (principal components) and eigenvalues (variance explained). SVD directly decomposes X = UΣVᵀ. Crucially, XᵀX = VΣ²Vᵀ, so V's columns are the principal components and σᵢ² are n·λᵢ. Computing PCA via SVD is more numerically stable because it avoids forming XᵀX (which squares the condition number). The principal component scores (projected data) are UΣ = XV — this is what you get from pca.fit_transform(X). In sklearn's PCA implementation with svd_solver='auto', it uses randomized SVD for large matrices or the LAPACK SVD for small ones. The computational cost of full SVD is O(min(n²d, nd²)), while truncated SVD (keep only top k) can be O(ndk) with randomized methods.

[Interview Angle] Q3: "Explain the difference between cross-entropy and KL divergence." — OpenAI L5

Cross-entropy H(p,q) = -∫p(x)·log q(x)dx measures the average number of bits needed to encode samples from p using a code optimized for q. KL divergence D_KL(p||q) = ∫p(x)·log(p(x)/q(x))dx = H(p,q) - H(p) measures the extra bits needed beyond the optimal encoding. Since H(p) is constant w.r.t. the model parameters, minimizing cross-entropy is equivalent to minimizing KL divergence. In ML practice, we minimize cross-entropy, which implicitly minimizes KL. Fun fact: KL divergence is NOT symmetric — D_KL(p||q) ≠ D_KL(q||p). The forward KL (p||q) is mode-averaging (mass-covering); the reverse KL (q||p) is mode-seeking (zero-avoiding). This explains why minimizing D_KL(p_data||p_model) in VI gives blurry images (mode-averaging), while GANs implicitly minimize reverse KL (mode-seeking).

[Interview Angle] Q4: "Derive the gradient of the ridge regression loss." — Google L5

Ridge regression loss: L(w) = ||Xw - y||² + λ||w||². Write as: (Xw-y)ᵀ(Xw-y) + λwᵀw. Expand: wᵀXᵀXw - 2wᵀXᵀy + yᵀy + λwᵀw. Take gradient w.r.t. w: ∇L = 2XᵀXw - 2Xᵀy + 2λw. Factor: ∇L = 2(XᵀX + λI)w - 2Xᵀy. Set to zero: (XᵀX + λI)w = Xᵀy → ŵ = (XᵀX + λI)⁻¹Xᵀy. Compare to OLS: (XᵀX)⁻¹Xᵀy. The λI term adds λ to each eigenvalue of XᵀX, which: (1) makes the matrix invertible even if XᵀX is singular (when features are collinear or n < p), and (2) shrinks coefficients toward zero by reducing each eigenvalue's contribution. The gradient w.r.t. a single coefficient wⱼ: ∂L/∂wⱼ = 2Xⱼᵀ(Xw - y) + 2λwⱼ, where Xⱼ is the j-th feature column.

[Interview Angle] Q5: "What's the p-value and what's a common misinterpretation?" — Netflix L4

A p-value is the probability of observing data as extreme as what was actually observed, assuming the null hypothesis H₀ is true. Formally: p = P(D ≥ d_obs | H₀). The most common misinterpretation: "p = 0.03 means there's a 3% chance H₀ is true" — this is WRONG. The p-value conditions on H₀; it says nothing about P(H₀|data). For that, you need Bayesian methods (posterior probability). The second common mistake: p=0.05 is not magic — it's a conventional threshold, not a wall. A p=0.049 and p=0.051 are practically indistinguishable. Third: multiple testing inflates false positives. If you run 20 independent tests with α=0.05, you expect 1 false positive by chance. Use Bonferroni or FDR correction. In ML, p-values are commonly used in A/B testing to determine if a treatment effect is statistically significant, but they don't tell you about effect size or practical significance — always report confidence intervals and effect sizes alongside p-values.

sections.title: References
sections.description:
• CS229 Lecture Notes — Andrew Ng: probability, linear algebra, optimization
  • PRML — Bishop (2006): Pattern Recognition and Machine Learning
  • MIT Linear Algebra (Gilbert Strang) — definitive SVD treatment
  • All of Statistics — Wasserman (2004): concise statistics reference
  • Elements of Information Theory — Cover & Thomas: information theory Bible
  • Convex Optimization — Boyd & Vandenberghe: free online, definitive text
